Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>針對 draft streaming 修改後，我發現還是有二個問題：
1.當上面的訊息增加時，原本已顯示在最下方的訊息突然減少字數，
例如原本"✅ Read File (2.7s) ✅ Read File (2.6s)
💭 …ack. Let me check if the onChange callback re-applies the model.
目前在驗證straming"
又變成"✅ Read File (2.7s) ✅ Read File (2.6s) ✅ Terminal (8.1s)
💭 …ack. Let me check if the onChange callback re-applies the model.
目前在驗證str"
2.整個訊息突然消失，過一會又出現</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-10</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
今天（2026-07-10）共 13 個 commit，加上一個未 commit 的文檔改動。整體分四條主線：

**1. Draft API streaming（今天的主戲，18:21–19:32，7 個 commit）**
- `e3a3a45` feat：實作 Bot API 10.1 的 `sendMessageDraft`/`sendRichMessageDraft` streaming 路徑——`telegram-rich-renderer.ts` 加 `trySendDraft()`、`run-prompt.ts` 建三階段 draft lifecycle（初始 draft → 每幀全量替換 → 最後 `sendRichMessage` 持久化）、新增 `TG_DRAFT_ENABLED` 開關 + 24 個 assertion 的 smoke test + SPEC 文檔。
- 之後連續 6 個 bugfix 收斂邊角：placeholder guard 排除 draft mode（`df788dc`）、error handler 與 `/cancel` 支援 draft（`7fc3fec`）、renderReply 截斷時扣 tools/thinking overhead（`718798e`）、HTML 膨脹超 4096 fallback 純文字（`f9670c6`）、draft TTL 過期降級 placeholder（`75a5428`）、draft+placeholder 雙失敗的死狀態修復（`7c72932`）。後三個都在防同一類問題：**訊息在 rate limit / 失敗情境下從使用者畫面消失**。

**2. Status web UI 重寫（17:17–17:29，3 個 commit）**
- 移除 Electron，改用 Telegram `web_app` 按鈕 + `STATUS_HTTPS_URL` fallback。
- `status-server.ts` 加 6 個 specialist API endpoint（設定/preamble/歷史/產出/facts）。
- `status-app/index.html` 重寫為 hash-routing SPA（Dashboard / Specialist Detail / Live View + SSE）。
- 另外 commit 了 specialist dashboard 的設計規格與實作計畫兩份文檔。

**3. Preamble 強化（15:39）**
- `8a2df86`：RELAY_DELEGATE / PARALLEL_DELEGATE 的 goal prompt 從三要素擴到五要素（加「已知背景」「待決問題」），借鏡 fable-advisor 的 focused-brief pattern。

**4. 零星修復與文檔**
- `.md` 檔送 Telegram 改顯示為 `.txt` 避免中文亂碼（08:12）。
- pending-roadmap 更新（skill 候選 4→6、Ruflo watchlist、Warm Pool）。

**未 commit**：`docs/usage-guide.html`（+31/-3），補三塊文檔——sessionreflect 的分類說明（fact / skill-candidate / global-principle）、`<<WIKI_QUERY>>` token 格式、以及「預設 11 個 skill 自動安裝 + pre-commit hook 自動同步 default-skills/」的說明。內容看起來是完整的，應該只是還沒 commit。

需要我把這個文檔改動 commit 掉，或者針對 draft streaming 那條線做進一步驗證（跑 `check-draft-streaming.mjs`）嗎?

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user in the primary session is reporting two issues after modifying draft streaming:

1. When messages are added at the top, messages displayed at the bottom suddenly show fewer characters. For example, text that originally showed "✅ Read File (2.7s) ✅ Read File (2.6s) 💭 …ack. Let me check if the onChange callback re-applies the model. 目前在驗證straming" becomes "✅ Read File (2.7s) ✅ Read File (2.6s) ✅ Terminal (8.1s) 💭 …ack. Let me check if the onChange callback re-applies the model. 目前在驗證str" (text gets truncated)

2. The entire message suddenly disappears and then reappears after a while.

This is a bug report about streaming message display issues - specifically about text truncation and message flickering/disappearing during streaming updates.

This qualifies as an observation because it describes a discovered problem with the draft streaming implementation. The type should be "discovery" since it's identifying issues with existing behavior, or potentially "bugfix" if we're recording that these are known issues to be fixed.

Let me structure this as a discovery observation since the user is identifying/reporting problems that exist after the draft streaming modifications.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Draft Streaming Display Issues Identified</title>
  <subtitle>Two rendering problems found in streaming message display: text truncation when new messages appear and intermittent message disappearance</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>When messages are added above, previously displayed messages at the bottom show fewer characters than before</fact>
    <fact>Example truncation: "目前在驗證straming" becomes "目前在驗證str" when new content appears above</fact>
    <fact>Entire messages intermittently disappear and reappear during streaming</fact>
    <fact>Issues occur after recent draft streaming modifications</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>After implementing draft streaming modifications, two critical display issues were identified. First, when new messages are added to the top of the message list, existing messages displayed at the bottom experience unexpected text truncation - content that was fully visible suddenly shows fewer characters. Second, complete messages intermittently vanish from the display and reappear moments later. These issues suggest potential problems with how the streaming update mechanism handles DOM updates, viewport calculations, or state synchronization during incremental content rendering.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```

content.thinking:
The user is investigating the draft streaming bug. This grep shows the draft mode logic in run-prompt.ts:

1. useDraftMode and draftId are local variables
2. Draft mode is initialized by sending an empty draft with a timestamp-based draftId
3. During streaming, if useDraftMode is true, it calls trySendDraft with the current content
4. If draft fails, it falls back to non-draft mode (placeholder)
5. There's logic to handle rate limit scenarios

This is examining the draft lifecycle mechanism to understand why messages might truncate or disappear.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Draft Mode Lifecycle and Fallback Logic</title>
  <subtitle>Draft streaming uses timestamp-based draft IDs with fallback to placeholder mode on failure</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>useDraftMode and draftId are local variables tracking draft state per streaming session</fact>
    <fact>draftId generated as Date.now() & 0x7FFFFFFF to ensure positive 32-bit integer</fact>
    <fact>Draft mode initialized by sending empty draft with trySendDraft, enables only if successful</fact>
    <fact>Each streaming update calls trySendDraft with full content when useDraftMode is true</fact>
    <fact>Draft failure triggers fallback: useDraftMode set to false, draftId reset to 0</fact>
    <fact>Rate limit scenario generates new draftId for retry instead of entering dead state</fact>
    <fact>Placeholder creation failure triggers useDraftMode path as alternative rendering mechanism</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The draft streaming implementation in run-prompt.ts manages a two-mode rendering strategy. Initially, it attempts to enable draft mode by sending an empty draft message with a timestamp-based ID. If successful, subsequent streaming updates send the complete content via trySendDraft. When draft operations fail (rate limits, API errors), the system falls back to placeholder mode by disabling useDraftMode. Special handling prevents dead states during rate limits by generating new draft IDs for retries. The dual-mode approach (draft vs placeholder) with runtime fallback logic may be contributing to the observed truncation and disappearance issues if state transitions aren't properly synchronized with the rendering layer.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is reading the full renderReply function implementation. This shows the complete logic for how streaming messages are rendered, including:

1. Tool lines are gathered first
2. Thinking buffer is shown during streaming (last ~200 chars as preview)
3. Body text is extracted from session.buffer
4. Overhead calculation from tools + thinkingBlock + cursor
5. Body is truncated to fit within TG_MAX (3900) minus overhead
6. Final content is tools + thinkingBlock + body + cursor
7. Converted to HTML via markdownToHtml
8. If HTML > 4096, fallback to plain text

This is relevant to understanding the truncation bug. The key insight here is that the overhead calculation is dynamic - it changes based on the length of tools, thinkingBlock, and cursor. If the overhead increases (e.g., more tool calls are added), the bodyBudget shrinks, causing the body text to be truncated more aggressively.

This could explain the bug: when new messages appear above (which might be new tool calls in the toolTimeline), the overhead increases, the bodyBudget decreases, and body.slice(-bodyBudget) takes fewer characters from the end, making it appear that text is disappearing.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>RenderReply Dynamic Truncation Mechanism Analyzed</title>
  <subtitle>Message body budget recalculates every render based on variable overhead from tools, thinking blocks, and cursor</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>renderReply constructs message as tools + thinkingBlock + body + cursor components</fact>
    <fact>Thinking block shows last 200 chars of thinkingBuffer during streaming, hidden when done</fact>
    <fact>Overhead recalculated on every render as tools.length + thinkingBlock.length + cursor.length</fact>
    <fact>Body budget formula: Math.max(TG_MAX - overhead, 200) where TG_MAX is 3900</fact>
    <fact>Body truncated as "…" + body.slice(-bodyBudget) when exceeding budget</fact>
    <fact>HTML fallback to plain text when markdownToHtml output exceeds 4096 bytes</fact>
    <fact>Tool lines generated from session.toolTimeline via toolPrefixLines function</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The renderReply function's truncation logic is fully dynamic - on every streaming update, it recalculates overhead based on the current lengths of tools, thinking blocks, and cursor elements, then adjusts the body budget accordingly. This explains the reported truncation bug: when new tool calls are added to the toolTimeline during streaming, the tools prefix grows longer, increasing overhead and shrinking bodyBudget. The body.slice(-bodyBudget) operation then takes fewer characters from the end, making previously visible text disappear. The thinking block also dynamically appears/disappears based on streaming state, further affecting the overhead calculation. This variable-budget approach creates unstable text rendering where the same body content shows different amounts based on what other components are present.</narrative>

    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

content.thinking:
The user is showing me code from run-prompt.ts that demonstrates:

1. The draft mode initialization logic (lines 407-422):
   - Creates draftId from Date.now()
   - Tries to send an empty draft
   - If successful, sets useDraftMode = true
   - Otherwise falls back to placeholder

2. The editNow() function (lines 445-495):
   - Calls renderReply to generate content
   - Compares with lastRendered to avoid duplicate edits
   - If in draft mode, calls trySendDraft with the full content
   - If draft fails, tries to create a placeholder as fallback
   - If that also fails, generates a new draftId for retry

The key insight here is that the editNow() function calls renderReply() on every streaming update, and renderReply() recalculates the overhead and bodyBudget each time. As the tool timeline grows (new tools being added), the overhead increases, the bodyBudget shrinks, and body.slice(-bodyBudget) takes fewer characters - causing the visible text to shrink.

This is the root cause of the truncation bug! The body budget is dynamically recalculated based on current overhead, so as overhead grows, the body shrinks.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Root Cause Identified: Dynamic Body Budget During Streaming</title>
  <subtitle>Each streaming update recalculates body budget based on growing overhead, causing previously rendered text to shrink as tool timeline expands</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>editNow() function calls renderReply on every streaming update to generate display content</fact>
    <fact>renderReply recalculates overhead as tools.length + thinkingBlock.length + cursor.length on each call</fact>
    <fact>When session.toolTimeline grows with new tool calls, tools string expands via toolPrefixLines</fact>
    <fact>Expanded tools increases overhead, which decreases bodyBudget via Math.max(TG_MAX - overhead, 200)</fact>
    <fact>Smaller bodyBudget causes body.slice(-bodyBudget) to take fewer characters from end of buffer</fact>
    <fact>Content comparison if (content === lastRendered) prevents redundant edits but not budget-driven truncation</fact>
    <fact>Draft mode sends full recalculated content via trySendDraft on each update without budget stabilization</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The root cause of the message truncation bug has been identified. The streaming update flow calls renderReply() on every frame to generate display content. renderReply() dynamically recalculates the overhead (tools + thinking + cursor lengths) and derives bodyBudget as TG_MAX minus that overhead. As the streaming session progresses and new tool calls are added to session.toolTimeline, the tools prefix grows longer, increasing overhead and shrinking the available body budget. Since the body is truncated via body.slice(-bodyBudget), a smaller budget means fewer characters are taken from the end of the buffer - making previously visible text disappear. The editNow() function sends this shrinking content to Telegram without any mechanism to stabilize the budget or preserve already-rendered text length. This creates the user-reported behavior where messages at the bottom show fewer characters when new tool results appear above.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
    <file>src/telegram-rich-renderer.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

While sendMessageDraft (API 9.3) and sendRichMessageDraft (API 10.1) are mentioned as methods for streaming partial messages, the changelog provides zero technical specifications - no parameter details, length constraints, TTL behavior, or overflow handling guidance. The implementation in telegram-kiro-bridge assumes 30-second TTL based on empirical observation and applies the same TG_MAX=3900 limit used for regular messages. This documentation void means the system is operating on assumptions rather than specifications, and any mismatch between assumed and actual API constraints could manifest as the observed truncation and disappearance bugs.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Draft Message Method Constraints Partially Clarified</title>
  <subtitle>sendMessageDraft confirmed for streaming with 4096 character limit but no official TTL specification found</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Telegram Bot API message length limit is 4096 characters for all message types</fact>
    <fact>sendMessageDraft introduced in Bot API 9.3 (Dec 31, 2025), available to all bots in 9.5 (March 1, 2026)</fact>
    <fact>sendMessageDraft is purpose-built for streaming partial messages during generation</fact>
    <fact>Draft updates may support faster intervals than editMessageText workaround</fact>
    <fact>Batching strategy for live draft updates is bot-controlled (per N chars or M seconds)</fact>
    <fact>No official TTL specification found in search results despite being mentioned in code comments</fact>
    <fact>Current implementation uses TG_MAX=3900 which is 196 characters below the 4096 limit</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Web search clarified some constraints for draft streaming. The standard 4096-character limit applies to all Telegram messages including drafts. sendMessageDraft is officially designed for streaming partial messages with potentially faster update rates than the editMessageText pattern. However, the 30-second TTL mentioned in the code comments remains unconfirmed by official documentation. The implementation's TG_MAX=3900 provides a 196-character safety margin below the 4096 limit, but this doesn't address the core issue: the dynamic overhead calculation in renderReply() causes bodyBudget to shrink as tools accumulate, making previously visible text disappear during streaming. The problem isn't the absolute limit but rather the unstable budget that changes on every render frame.</narrative>

  <narrative>The AI features documentation reveals critical constraints for draft message streaming. Drafts have a definite TTL controlled by message_typing_draft_ttl (confirming the code's 30-second assumption) and expire when that timer runs out or when any regular message arrives. More importantly, aggressive rate limiting permits only 20 draft updates in 5 seconds or 40 in 30 seconds per chat. Violations trigger FLOOD_WAIT errors that block further updates. These constraints directly explain the second reported bug - intermittent message disappearance. When drafts expire due to TTL or when rate limits are hit, the message vanishes from the UI. The system's fallback mechanism (attempt placeholder creation on draft failure) may not execute quickly enough, creating the observed disappearance-then-reappearance pattern as fallback messages are sent.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Draft Streaming Implementation Specification Review</title>
  <subtitle>Three-phase lifecycle design with fallback chains documented but lacks handling for dynamic overhead growth during streaming</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Design uses three-phase lifecycle: sendMessageDraft with empty text → sendRichMessageDraft updates → sendRichMessage finalization</fact>
    <fact>Draft API limited to private chats only with 30-second TTL ephemeral messages</fact>
    <fact>Fallback chain: draft failure returns to placeholder+editMessageText pattern</fact>
    <fact>Rate limit recovery attempts to rebuild draft using same draftId</fact>
    <fact>renderReply() explicitly noted as unchanged - continues to rebuild entire buffer on each frame</fact>
    <fact>Implementation totaled approximately 240 lines across telegram-rich-renderer.ts, run-prompt.ts, config.ts, and tests</fact>
    <fact>Specification includes 8 behavior contracts but none address stable text rendering during overhead growth</fact>
    <fact>Multi-chunk handling: first chunk uses sendRichMessage (persistent), subsequent chunks use richSendOrQueue</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Review of SPEC-draft-streaming.md reveals the original design focused on API integration and fallback mechanisms but did not anticipate the dynamic truncation issue. The specification explicitly states "renderReply() — 仍然 rebuild 整個 buffer" (renderReply still rebuilds entire buffer), treating it as an unchanged component. However, this design assumption creates the truncation bug: as the streaming session progresses, toolTimeline growth increases overhead in renderReply's calculation, shrinking bodyBudget and causing previously visible text to disappear. The spec's behavior contracts verify token stripping, 429 recovery, and API integration but include no requirement for stable text length preservation during streaming. The 30s TTL and rate limit recovery mechanism also don't account for the disappearance pattern when drafts expire without successful fallback execution.</narrative>
  <concepts>

    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>trade-off</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>

```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Streaming Update Interval Configuration</title>
  <subtitle>Default 1.2-second throttle on editNow calls limits render frequency but doesn't prevent dynamic overhead growth between updates</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS defaults to 1200ms via environment variable or config</fact>
    <fact>Minimum interval enforced at 500ms via Math.max(500, configured_value)</fact>
    <fact>Throttling implemented in session.onUpdate callback with elapsed time check and setTimeout</fact>
    <fact>editNow() fires when elapsed >= STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS or via deferred timer</fact>
    <fact>Same throttling pattern appears in multiple locations: index.ts line 953, run-prompt.ts lines 519 and 1506</fact>
    <fact>Throttle reduces API call frequency but each call still executes full renderReply with dynamic budget recalculation</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The streaming update mechanism uses a 1.2-second default interval to throttle editNow() calls, preventing excessive API requests. While this reduces the frequency of updates, it doesn't address the core truncation issue - each throttled update still calls renderReply(), which dynamically recalculates overhead and bodyBudget based on the current toolTimeline size. As tool calls accumulate over multiple 1.2-second intervals, each renderReply execution sees progressively larger overhead and returns progressively smaller body text. The throttling merely slows down the visible truncation rather than preventing it. Combined with the 20-calls-per-5-seconds rate limit from Telegram's draft API, the system operates in a narrow window where too-frequent updates trigger FLOOD_WAIT errors but each permitted update still exhibits unstable text length.</narrative>
  <concepts>

    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

content.thinking:
This is important information about how Telegram clients handle draft message rendering. The key finding is that clients calculate the number of characters to animate as max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev)), which means they only animate NEW characters added. But the implementation is doing FULL REPLACEMENT of the draft content each time, which could cause issues if the new content is SHORTER than the previous content (negative character count).

This explains the truncation behavior! When the body budget shrinks due to overhead growth, the new content is SHORTER than the previous content. The animation formula max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev)) would give 0, meaning no animation. But more importantly, the client is receiving a SHORTER message than before, which could manifest as text disappearing.

Also confirms the message_typing_draft_ttl automatic deletion behavior - drafts disappear after TTL seconds OR when a normal message arrives.

```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Telegram Client Draft Animation Behavior Explains Truncation</title>
  <subtitle>Clients animate only new characters based on length difference; shorter replacement drafts cause visible text disappearance</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Telegram clients animate draft updates by fading in or animating only added characters</fact>
    <fact>Animation character count calculated as max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev))</fact>
    <fact>Formula only animates when new text is LONGER than previous text</fact>
    <fact>When strlen(new) is less than strlen(prev), animation count becomes 0</fact>
    <fact>Drafts automatically deleted by clients after message_typing_draft_ttl seconds from reception</fact>
    <fact>Drafts also deleted immediately when normal message received in same chat</fact>
    <fact>Documentation does not specify client behavior for text insertions at beginning or mid-content replacements</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Critical discovery about Telegram's draft rendering mechanism explains the truncation bug. Clients calculate animated characters as max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev)), meaning they only visually animate ADDITIONS. When the system sends a replacement draft where the new content is SHORTER than the previous version (which happens when bodyBudget shrinks due to overhead growth), the formula produces 0 animated characters. The client receives genuinely shorter text, causing previously visible content to disappear from the display. This isn't a client rendering glitch - the bot is actually sending progressively shorter messages as renderReply's dynamic budget calculation reduces the body.slice(-bodyBudget) window. The full-replacement draft strategy (same draftId with complete new text) combined with shrinking content creates the user-reported experience of text "突然減少字數" (suddenly showing fewer characters).</narrative>

content.thinking:
This read shows the final delivery mechanism for messages after streaming completes. The key observations:

1. "雙失敗兜底" (dual-failure safety net) at line 1008: if both draft expires AND placeholder creation failed, it tries one last-resort placeholder creation, and if that also fails, it switches to useDraftMode path (direct sendRichMessage/sendMessage)

2. Single chunk handling (lines 1063-1108):
   - Draft path: uses trySendRichMessage to persist (draft auto-disappears)
   - Placeholder path: uses tryEditRichMessageDraft or editMessageText to finalize the placeholder

3. Multi-chunk handling (lines 1120+):
   - First chunk: sendRichMessage (draft disappears) or editMessageText (placeholder)
   - Subsequent chunks follow with richSendOrQueue

This shows the finalization mechanism but doesn't address the streaming truncation issue. The problem occurs BEFORE this final delivery - during the streaming phase where editNow() is called repeatedly with shrinking bodyBudget values.

The "雙失敗兜底" mechanism is trying to handle the case where draft expires but doesn't address why the text content shrinks during streaming.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Final Delivery Safety Net and Multi-Path Finalization</title>
  <subtitle>Dual-failure fallback creates last-resort placeholder if both draft expires and initial placeholder failed</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Dual-failure safety net at line 1008 handles case where draft expired and no placeholder exists</fact>
    <fact>Last-resort attempt creates minimal placeholder with "…" text before final delivery</fact>
    <fact>If last-resort placeholder also fails, system switches to useDraftMode direct send path</fact>
    <fact>Single-chunk finalization: draft path uses trySendRichMessage to persist, placeholder path uses edit</fact>
    <fact>Multi-chunk finalization: first chunk persists via sendRichMessage or editMessageText, remaining chunks use richSendOrQueue</fact>
    <fact>Draft finalization causes draft message to automatically disappear per Telegram API behavior</fact>
    <fact>Final message includes reply_markup with ASK keyboard only on last chunk or single-chunk messages</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The final delivery mechanism includes a dual-failure safety net addressing the second reported bug (messages disappearing). When a draft expires due to the 30-second TTL and no placeholder exists, the system makes a last-resort attempt to create a minimal placeholder before finalizing content. This explains the "過一會又出現" (reappears after a while) behavior - the message disappears when the draft expires, then reappears when the last-resort placeholder is created or when the final sendRichMessage persists the content. However, this safety net doesn't prevent the disappearance itself, only reduces its duration. The finalization flow correctly handles draft-to-persistent transition but operates on whatever content was last rendered - if that content was already truncated due to shrinking bodyBudget during streaming, the finalized message preserves the truncated version.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
This is a smoke test file that validates the draft streaming implementation. It tests various scenarios:
1. Plain draft with empty text vs content
2. Rich draft mode
3. CRLF normalization
4. API unavailability fallback
5. API error handling
6. Same draftId reuse for full-replacement updates
7. Final rich message persistence

The test confirms the implementation works at the API integration level but doesn't test the renderReply dynamic budget issue that causes truncation. This is purely a unit test for the telegram-rich-renderer functions, not an end-to-end test of the streaming behavior.

This is relevant as it shows the implementation was tested for API correctness but not for the content stability issue during streaming.

    <fact>Test validates fallback behavior when API methods unavailable or throw errors</fact>

  <narrative>The smoke test thoroughly validates the draft API integration layer - method signatures, parameter passing, error handling, and the full-replacement update pattern. It confirms the implementation correctly calls sendRichMessageDraft with the same draftId on each frame, which is the intended design for streaming updates. However, the test suite operates purely at the telegram-rich-renderer module level and does not exercise the renderReply → editNow → trySendDraft pipeline that exhibits the truncation bug. No test validates content stability when overhead grows during a multi-frame streaming session. The test passes because the API integration is correct; the bug manifests at a higher level where renderReply's dynamic budget calculation interacts with the draft replacement mechanism.</narrative>

content.thinking:
This is a significant code change! The user is implementing a new function `renderDraftReply` to address the truncation bug. The key insight here is explained in the extensive Chinese comments:

The fix recognizes that Telegram clients animate draft updates based on length difference: they immediately display the first strlen(prev) characters, then fade in the last max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev)) characters. This assumes append-only content. When new tool lines complete and are inserted at the head, the client shows fewer body characters, creating the backwards effect.

The solution: **put volatile regions (thinking/tools) AFTER the body instead of before**, so each frame only appends at the tail.

Structure comparison:
- Old renderReply: `tools + thinkingBlock + body + cursor`
- New renderDraftReply: `body + cursor + statusTail` where statusTail = `thinkingBlock + tools`

This is a critical bugfix that directly addresses the root cause identified earlier.

<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>New renderDraftReply Function Reorders Components for Append-Only Streaming</title>
  <subtitle>Moves volatile thinking/tools sections after body to prevent text truncation when new tool results appear</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>New renderDraftReply function added to telegram-ui.ts for draft streaming display</fact>
    <fact>Component order changed from tools+thinking+body+cursor to body+cursor+statusTail</fact>
    <fact>statusTail combines thinking and tools sections, placed after body content</fact>
    <fact>Design addresses Telegram client animation assumption of append-only content updates</fact>
    <fact>Client animates max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev)) characters at the end</fact>
    <fact>When tools inserted at head in old order, client truncates body end causing visible text regression</fact>
    <fact>New order ensures volatile regions grow at tail, making each frame append-only</fact>
    <fact>Returns markdown text not HTML for sendRichMessageDraft rich_message.markdown parameter</fact>
    <fact>Empty content returns empty string triggering server-native "Thinking…" placeholder</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Critical bugfix implemented by adding renderDraftReply as a draft-optimized alternative to renderReply. The root cause analysis revealed that Telegram clients animate draft updates using a length-difference algorithm that assumes append-only content - they immediately show the first strlen(prev) characters, then fade in new characters at the end. The original renderReply places tools and thinking blocks BEFORE the body, so when new tool results arrive and expand the prefix, the body budget shrinks and fewer body characters fit. The client receives genuinely shorter total text but with a longer prefix, causing visible body text to disappear from the end. The fix reorders components to body+cursor+statusTail, placing the volatile thinking/tools regions AFTER the stable body content. This ensures that as overhead grows, the growth happens at the tail of the message where Telegram's animation expects it, creating an append-only appearance. Each frame appears to add content at the end rather than insert at the beginning, eliminating the truncation effect users experienced.</narrative>
  <concepts>

    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

content.thinking:
This is a major edit that integrates renderDraftReply into the editNow() streaming update function and adds a draft keepalive mechanism. Let me break down what changed:

1. **Added lastDraftSentAt tracker** to timestamp successful draft sends
2. **Refactored editNow() logic**:
   - Draft path now comes FIRST and uses renderDraftReply instead of renderReply
   - renderDraftReply returns markdown string (not HTML) with body+cursor+statusTail ordering
   - Tracks lastDraftSentAt on successful draft send
   - On draft failure, falls back with renderReply for placeholder creation
   - Clears lastRendered when downgrading from draft to placeholder to avoid format mismatch
3. **Added draft keepalive timer**:
   - Runs every 10 seconds when useDraftMode is active
   - Forces a resend if ≥10s since last successful draft send
   - Prevents 30s TTL expiration during long tool execution or rate limit pauses
   - Bypasses identical content check by clearing lastRendered

This directly addresses both reported bugs:
- Bug 1 (truncation): renderDraftReply reorders components so body is first, preventing the "head insertion causes tail truncation" issue
- Bug 2 (disappearance): keepalive timer ensures draft is refreshed before 30s TTL expires

This is a comprehensive bugfix implementation.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Integrated renderDraftReply and Added Draft Keepalive Timer</title>
  <subtitle>Draft path now uses append-only rendering and prevents TTL expiration with 10-second keepalive refreshes</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>editNow() refactored to use renderDraftReply for draft mode before falling back to renderReply</fact>
    <fact>Draft path moved to front of editNow logic with early return on success</fact>
    <fact>renderDraftReply returns markdown string with body-first component ordering</fact>
    <fact>Added lastDraftSentAt timestamp tracking for successful draft sends</fact>
    <fact>Draft keepalive timer created with 10-second interval (DRAFT_KEEPALIVE_MS constant)</fact>
    <fact>Keepalive forces resend if ≥10 seconds since last successful draft transmission</fact>
    <fact>Keepalive bypasses identical content check by clearing lastRendered to empty string</fact>
    <fact>Worst-case keepalive interval is 20 seconds, well below 30-second TTL threshold</fact>
    <fact>Keepalive skips execution when turn finalized, rate limited, or draft mode disabled</fact>
    <fact>Draft failure downgrade clears lastRendered to prevent markdown/HTML format mismatch</fact>
    <fact>Placeholder creation on draft failure now uses renderReply for HTML formatting</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The core bugfix integrates the new renderDraftReply function into the streaming update pipeline and adds a keepalive mechanism to prevent draft expiration. The editNow() function now prioritizes draft mode, calling renderDraftReply which returns markdown text with the append-only component ordering (body+cursor+statusTail). This addresses the first bug where tool growth caused visible text truncation - by placing volatile sections after the body, all growth happens at the tail where Telegram's length-difference animation expects it. A critical addition is the draft keepalive timer that runs every 10 seconds during active draft streaming. When 10+ seconds pass without a draft send (due to long tool execution, rate limits, or unchanged content), it forces a resend to refresh the 30-second TTL. This solves the second bug where messages disappeared during quiet periods. The implementation carefully tracks lastDraftSentAt on successful sends, clears lastRendered during mode transitions to prevent format mismatches, and uses proper guards to prevent keepalive execution after turn finalization.</narrative>

    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

</observation>
